1: Psalm 58 is a sharp indictment of corrupt judges. The superscription: אַל-תַּשְׁחֵת (ʾal-tashḥêt, "Do Not Destroy") — the same tune as Psalms 57 and 59. The opening address: הַאֻמְנָם אֵלֶם צֶדֶק תְּדַבֵּרוּן (haʾumnām ʾêlem ṣedeq tĕdabbêrûn, "Do you indeed decree what is right, you rulers?") — ʾêlem: the addressees are either ʾêlîm (gods/mighty ones) or ʾêlem (the silent/mute ones — those who should speak but remain silent on justice). The divine council theme (Ps 82) may be in view: heavenly beings or earthly judges who fail their mandate. מֵישָׁרִים תִּשְׁפְּטוּ בְּנֵי אָדָם (mêshārîm tishpĕṭû bĕnê ʾādām, "Do you judge human beings with justice?") — mêshārîm: uprightness, what is straight (from yāshar: straight, right). The judicial standard they are failing.
2: אַף בְּלֵב עוֹלֹת תִּפְעָלוּן (ʾap bĕlêb ʿawlōt tipʿālûn, "No — in your heart you devise wrongs") — ʿawlāh: injustice, perversity (the twisted/crooked as opposed to the straight); pāʿal: to work, to do — the judicial injustice is their active labor. בָּאָרֶץ חֲמַס יְדֵיכֶם תְּפַלֵּסוּן (bāʾāreṣ ḥāmās yĕdêkem tĕpallêsûn, "your hands administer violence throughout the land") — ḥāmās: violence (the pre-Flood crime of Gen 6:11 — "the earth was full of violence/ḥāmās"); pālas (piel: to weigh out, to balance — as on a scale): the metaphor of justice perverted: instead of weighing out righteousness, the judges are weighing out violence on the scales of justice.
3: זֹרוּ רְשָׁעִים מֵרָחֶם (zōrû rĕshāʿîm mêrāḥem, "The wicked are alienated from the womb") — zûr: to be a stranger, to be alienated, to turn aside. Mêrāḥem: "from the womb" (from reḥem: the womb/uterus — the place of origin). The wicked's estrangement from righteousness is not learned but congenital — from the very source of their existence. תָּעוּ מִבֶּטֶן דֹּבְרֵי כָזָב (tāʿû mibbeten dōbĕrê kāzāb, "from their very birth they go astray, speaking lies") — beten: belly, womb (the inner body); tāʿāh: to wander astray, to err. The newborn already lying — not a developmental disorder but an ontological condition. This is the Psalter's closest approach to the doctrine of original sin (alongside Ps 51:5: "I was sinful from birth"). Paul's argument in Rom 3:10-18 draws on exactly this Psalter tradition.
4: חֲמַת-לָמוֹ כִּדְמוּת חֲמַת-נָחָשׁ (ḥămat-lāmô kidmût ḥămat-nāḥāsh, "Their venom is like the venom of a snake") — ḥēmāh: venom, but also burning rage (the word for both poison and fury): the enemies have poison-anger. Nāḥāsh: the serpent — the animal whose poison is delivered through the mouth, just as the judges deliver injustice through speech. כְּמוֹ-פֶתֶן חֵרֵשׁ יַאְטֵם אָזְנוֹ (kĕmô-peten ḥêrêsh yaʾṭêm ʾoznô, "like the deaf cobra that blocks its ear") — peten: the cobra (specifically the spitting cobra); ḥêrêsh: deaf; ʾāṭam: to block, to stop up. The cobra that refuses to hear the charmer — a refusal of the divine appeal to repentance.
5: אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יִשְׁמַע לְקוֹל מְלַחֲשִׁים (ʾăsher lōʾ-yishmaʿ lĕqôl mĕlaḥăshîm, "that will not listen to the voice of the charmers") — lāḥash: to whisper, to charm (snake-charming involved whispered incantations); the cobra is deaf to the charmer's art. חוֹבֵר חֲבָרִים מְחֻכָּם (ḥôbêr ḥăbārîm mĕḥukkām, "no matter how skillfully they do it") — ḥābar (piel: to bind together, to make a spell): the binding-together of magical words; mĕḥukkām: skillfully, with wisdom. The charmer's skill is useless against the hardened ear. The image establishes the total moral imperviousness of the wicked judges to divine or prophetic appeal.
6: אֱלֹהִים הֲרָס-שִׁנֵּימוֹ בְּפִימוֹ (ʾĕlōhîm hăras-shinnêmô bĕpîmô, "Smash their teeth in their mouths, O God!") — hāras: to break down, to demolish (a construction term — as walls are demolished). The teeth as the instrument of the lions of v4 — the predatory weapon removed. מַלְתְּעוֹת כְּפִירִים נְתָץ יְהוָה (maltĕʿôt kĕpîrîm nĕtaṣ YHWH, "tear out the fangs of the young lions, O LORD!") — maltĕʿôt: the great jaw-teeth, the molars (the crushing teeth); kĕpîrîm: young lions in their prime strength. The removal of the judicial predators' weapons through divine force.
7: יִמָּאֲסוּ כְמוֹ-מַיִם יִתְהַלְּכוּ-לָמוֹ (yimmāʾăsû kĕmô-māyim yithalĕkû-lāmô, "Let them drain away like water flowing downhill") — māʾas: to melt away, to dissolve (like snow in the sun); hālak: to walk, to flow away. The enemies dissolved like water on a slope — no remnant, no holding. יִדְרֹךְ חִצָּיו כְּמוֹ יִתְמֹלָלוּ (yidrok ḥiṣṣāyw kĕmô yitmōlālû, "when they draw their bows, let their arrows fall harmless") — dārak: to tread, to draw (a bow — the foot on the bow to string it, or the drawing of the arrow); the image of the archer whose arrows become blunt/cut to pieces before they fly.
8: כְּמוֹ שַׁבְּלוּל תֶּמֶס יַהֲלֹךְ (kĕmô shabbĕlûl temes yahălōk, "Let them be like a snail that dissolves as it goes") — shabbĕlûl: a snail (a hapax legomenon — appears only here in the entire OT). The snail dissolving as it moves — the trail of moisture that was thought in antiquity to be the snail consuming itself as it traveled. נֵפֶל אֵשֶׁת בַּל-חָזוּ שָׁמֶשׁ (nêpel ʾêshet bal-ḥāzû shāmesh, "like a stillborn child that never sees the light of the sun") — nêpel: a miscarriage, a stillborn child (from nāpal: to fall). The stillborn who never opens its eyes to sunlight — complete non-existence. The three images (water, snail, stillborn) together describe total dissolution: the wicked unmade as if they never were.
9: בְּטֶרֶם יָבִינוּ סִּירֹתֵיכֶם אָטָד (bĕṭerem yābînû sîrōtêkem ʾāṭād, "Before your cooking pots have felt the heat of the thornwood") — sîr: a cooking pot; ʾāṭād: thornbush, bramble — used for quick-burning fuel. The image: before the thorns even heat the pot (almost instantaneously), the judgment comes — the speed of divine justice against the wicked. כְּמוֹ-חַי כְּמוֹ-חָרוֹן יִשְׂעָרֶנּוּ (kĕmô-ḥay kĕmô-ḥārôn yisʿārennû, "both the green and the burning, he will sweep them away") — the storm-wind (saʿar) taking both the living and the hot together — the total, immediate sweep of judgment.
10: יִשְׂמַח צַדִּיק כִּי-חָזָה נָקָם (yiśmaḥ ṣaddîq kî-ḥāzāh nāqām, "The righteous will rejoice when they see vengeance carried out") — one of the most difficult verses in the Psalter for modern readers. Nāqām: vengeance, retribution — the vindication of justice through punishment. ʿŊfôd. The righteous person's joy is not sadism but the satisfaction of justice finally established — the vindication of the wronged. פְּעָמָיו יִרְחַץ בְּדַם-הָרָשָׁע (pĕʿāmāyw yirḥaṣ bĕdam-hārāshāʿ, "they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked") — a visceral image of total victory (the warrior who walks through the blood of the battlefield — also Rev 19:13-15). The metaphor expresses absolute divine triumph over injustice, not a literal program.
11: וְיֹאמַר אָדָם אַךְ-פְּרִי לַצַּדִּיק (wĕyōʾmar ʾādām ʾak-pĕrî laṣṣaddîq, "Then people will say, 'There really is a reward for the righteous'") — pĕrî: fruit, reward (the fruit as the consequence of the right life). The public acknowledgment (ʾādām: humanity in general) that justice is real — that righteousness is not futile. The psalm's final aim: the vindication of justice itself in the public square. אַךְ יֵשׁ-אֱלֹהִים שֹׁפְטִים בָּאָרֶץ (ʾak yêsh-ʾĕlōhîm shōpĕṭîm bāʾāreṣ, "truly there is a God who judges the earth") — the cosmic counter-claim to the corrupt human court: above the judges who pervert justice stands the divine Judge who does not. The earth has a Judge.
